channel.title: AI Home Building
channel.description: Research, analysis, and reporting on how artificial intelligence is transforming home design, building, and project management.

item.title: An AI Scored Your Roof From a Satellite. Your Insurer Never Knocked.
item.description: Computer vision models are grading 38% of American roofs as moderate-to-poor condition from aerial imagery alone. The scores determine your premium, your renewability, and increasingly whether you get covered at all.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: Your Contractor Waited 7 Days to Strip Forms. The Concrete Was Ready in 3.
item.description: A $50 wireless sensor embedded in your foundation pour calculates real-time concrete strength using ASTM C1074 maturity curves. It replaces guesswork with data and saves 3-10 days of schedule.
item.author: Jake Kowalski

item.title: Your Builder Waited 11 Days for a Draw. The AI Approved the Next One in 9 Minutes.
item.description: The construction industry loses $273 billion a year to slow payments. AI draw agents are compressing week-long reviews into minutes. For the residential builder juggling three projects on one checking account, this matters more than any robot on the job site.
item.author: Frank DeLuca

item.title: AI Inspection Tools Write Faster Reports. They Don’t See Through Walls.
item.description: Every major AI home inspection startup is optimizing report generation, not defect detection. The ASHI Standards of Practice already prohibit inspectors from opening walls. AI processing visible-light photos of those same walls doesn’t change what’s hidden behind them.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: Your AI Kitchen Render Forgot About the Plumbing. The Contractor Didn't.
item.description: AI renovation visualization tools generate photorealistic rooms with zero structural knowledge. 70% of homeowners already go over budget. When the render becomes the brief, the gap between beautiful and buildable gets expensive fast.
item.author: Elena Vasquez

item.title: The No. 2 Lumber in Your Walls Might Not Be No. 2. AI Grading Is Fixing That.
item.description: Human lumber graders overestimate value by 20%, according to the USDA Forest Service. New AI vision systems at sawmills are 31% more accurate. For builders buying framing packages, the downstream math on callbacks and rework changes substantially.
item.author: Frank DeLuca

item.title: Your Insurer's AI Already Scored Your Home for Wildfire Risk. You Weren't Told.
item.description: ZestyAI found 1.2 million California homes rated HIGH risk by AI but LOW risk by FEMA. The construction features that move your score cost $2,800 to fix. The math on when that pays for itself is simpler than you think.
item.author: Jake Kowalski

item.title: Your City's AI Approved the Plans. The Foundation Cracked. Who Pays?
item.description: AI plan review cuts permitting from weeks to 30 minutes across 26 states. But when the algorithm misses a code violation, the liability chain collapses: vendors disclaim, municipalities claim immunity, architects point to the approval stamp. Homeowners are left holding the bill.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: Steel Tariffs Are Accidentally Decarbonizing Buildings. Lumber Tariffs Are Undoing It.
item.description: 50% steel tariffs push builders toward mass timber, cutting embodied carbon by 40%. But 35% lumber tariffs push them back. AI carbon-tracking tools could navigate this contradiction, but almost nobody uses them.
item.author: Priya Greenwood

item.title: The Industry Spent a Decade Teaching AI to Fly Drones and Drive Excavators. Nobody Taught It to Answer an RFI.
item.description: Construction professionals lose 14 hours a week to paperwork. AI research spends less than 1% of its attention on the problem. The $177 billion disconnect between where the money goes and where the time goes.
item.author: Frank DeLuca

item.title: You Installed a $500 Smart Water Valve for the Insurance Discount. The Fine Print Says Keep It Plugged In or Lose Coverage.
item.description: Insurance carriers are offering 5-10% premium discounts for smart water shutoff valves. What the brochure skips: protective device endorsements that make the discount a contractual obligation, not a reward.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: The Construction Industry Lost 1.4 Million Tradespeople in 18 Years. It Gained 800,000 Managers.
item.description: Microsoft and OpenAI are pouring money into AI training for construction workers. NABTU's programs reach 1,500 instructors. The industry has 7.1 million tradespeople. The math does not work, and the structural shift underneath it is worse than the training gap.
item.author: Marcus Washington

item.title: Your Energy Model Promised 25% Savings. Across 140,000 Homes, the Average Was 7%.
item.description: A systematic review of 140,977 retrofitted households found average energy savings of 7.2%, not the 20-30% that models predict. AI energy tools add decimal places to the same wrong number.
item.author: Priya Greenwood

item.title: Forty Cities Use AI to Review Building Permits. Nineteen Thousand Don't. The Math Isn't the Problem.
item.description: AI plan review cuts permit times by 50-70% where deployed. Fewer than 40 of America's 20,000 permit-issuing jurisdictions use it. The gap costs homebuyers an estimated $2.4 billion a year in avoidable delays.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: Your AI Scheduler Shaved Three Weeks Off Your Build. The Inspector Queue Added Four Back.
item.description: AI scheduling tools promise 20% faster construction timelines. But when 33-44% of your build calendar is government wait time, that optimization hits a hard ceiling. We ran the numbers.
item.author: Frank DeLuca

item.title: Your AI Floor Plan Has No Ceiling. Literally.
item.description: Zero consumer AI floor plan generators model ceiling height as a design variable. The vertical dimension — the thing that makes a room feel like a cathedral or a cave — is invisible to every tool homeowners are using to design their homes.
item.author: Elena Vasquez

item.title: ICON's $899,000 Titan Prints Your Walls for $20 a Square Foot. The Other 80% of Your House Still Costs the Same.
item.description: ICON launched the Titan Print System at $899K. The $20/sq ft wall claim is real, but walls are 20% of a home's cost. We ran the amortization math for builders at every scale.
item.author: Jake Kowalski

item.title: Your Green Home Scored a HERS 40. Its Embodied Carbon Is Identical to the Code-Minimum House Next Door.
item.description: A 100-home Massachusetts study found above-code homes are no better at limiting embodied carbon than standard builds. Your energy model optimizes half the problem.
item.author: Priya Greenwood

item.title: Your Builder Uses AI. Your Contract Doesn't Mention It. Guess Who Loses That Argument.
item.description: 49% of homebuilders now use AI tools. Zero standard construction contracts address what happens when those tools fail. The legal gap is real, and it's your problem.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: Your AI Kitchen Layout Passes the Eye Test. It Fails 27 of 31 Industry Design Rules.
item.description: AI kitchen design tools generate stunning layouts that violate the NKBA's 31 planning guidelines. Here's what they miss and what it costs you when the cabinets arrive.
item.author: Elena Vasquez

item.title: Your Builder Manages a $600K Project With the Same Software That Runs Your Fantasy Football League
item.description: Procore's new AI agents review submittals, file RFIs, and draft daily logs autonomously. They cost $21K a year. Most custom home builders can't break even on that until $2M in annual volume. The residential AI gap is now a canyon.
item.author: Frank DeLuca
